symptom: Outdoor unit is humming or clicking but the compressor isn't starting. Fan may or may not spin. In some cases cooling is intermittent — compressor starts, runs a few seconds, then trips out. Customer may report the AC 'trying' to start. Classic on hot afternoons after a long runtime cycle.
diagnosis: Failed or weak dual-run capacitor. A dual cap stores and delivers the phase-shifted current needed to start the compressor and run the condenser fan. When capacitance drops below spec (typically more than 6% off rated µF), the compressor can't achieve starting torque and trips its internal overload.
procedure: Kill power at the disconnect AND the breaker. Verify with a non-contact tester on the contactor.
Remove the access panel on the condenser (usually 4 sheet-metal screws).
Discharge the existing capacitor: short across HERM and C terminals with an insulated 20k-ohm resistor for 5 seconds, then C to FAN.
Photograph the existing wiring before disconnecting. Typical colors: Yellow to HERM (compressor), Brown to FAN, Black to C (common from contactor).
Check the data plate spec on the cap side: e.g. 45/5 µF 440V ±6%. HERM side is the high number, FAN side is the low number.
Test the old cap with a multimeter in capacitance mode. If either side reads >6% off spec, or the cap is visibly bulged or leaking, replace.
Match the replacement exactly: same µF, same or higher voltage rating (440V is common; 370V works on smaller systems).
Land wires on matching terminals. Secure with the original retaining bracket — a loose cap will vibrate and fail early.
Restore power. Listen for a clean start: no clicking, no hum. Verify 3 full cooling cycles before leaving.
Measure temp split at the return vs supply: should be 15-20F once equilibrated.
safety_notes: Capacitors store lethal voltage even when power is off. Always discharge before touching terminals. Never short them with a screwdriver — use a resistor.
Bulging or leaking caps indicate catastrophic failure. Wear gloves; the dielectric oil can irritate skin.
Pull the disconnect AND the breaker. Some installs have the contactor coil energized from a different circuit than the main.
If the new cap fails within minutes of install, the compressor may be drawing too much current — possible stuck or seized compressor. Stop and escalate; do not keep replacing caps.
